title: BufferedReader.take spanning path calls Array.shift() inside a per-chunk loop, making it O(n) in queue depth
description: In the spanning path of BufferedReader.take(), `this.chunks.shift()` (lines 138 and 151) is called each time a chunk is exhausted while coalescing bytes across chunks. Array.shift() is O(n) in array length because it re-indexes every remaining element. A caller that appends many small chunks (e.g. 4 KB network reads building up a 1 MB buffer) then calls take(total) will trigger O(k) shifts where k is the number of pending chunks, making the full coalesce O(k^2). The fix is a standard read-index pointer (already used on the first chunk via `this.head`) promoted to cover the full chunks array, or replacing the array with a ring-buffer/linked-list deque so removal from front is O(1).

title: parseNonNegativeInt compiles an inline regex literal on each call in engines without regex literal caching
description: parseNonNegativeInt uses `!/^\d+$/.test(s)` as an inline regex literal (line 815). V8 and SpiderMonkey typically cache regex literals per function activation, but JSC (Safari/WKWebView) and some embedded engines do not guarantee this. Since parseNonNegativeInt is called for every Content-Range and Content-Length header on every fetch (potentially many times in a tile-streaming pyramid app), the safe fix is to hoist the regex to a module-level constant so compilation is guaranteed once. The impact is negligible on V8 but measurable on Safari where JXL pyramid viewers run on iOS.

title: signal?.aborted checked twice per loop iteration in fromReadableStream hot loop via optional-chaining double-dereference
description: In fromReadableStream's inner loop (lines 98-131) and fromByteRange's inner loop (lines 603-649), `signal?.aborted` is evaluated once at the top of each iteration and again after `await session.push(chunk)`. For large files with many small network chunks (e.g. 64 KB reads over a 100 MB pyramid sidecar) this is hundreds of optional-chain dereferences per second. The `signal` reference is already stored in a local const, so the pattern `signal !== undefined && signal.aborted` or caching `const checkAbort = signal ? () => signal.aborted : () => false` eliminates the repeated property lookup on the optional chain. This is minor on V8 but measurable in tight prefetch loops on low-end devices.

title: makeInfo already captures ttfbMs and transferMs but no CI assertion enforces regression bounds on those metrics
description: fromByteRange carefully measures TTFB and transfer duration (lines 455-467, 670-671) and surfaces them via onRangeNegotiated. However, there is no test or CI hook that asserts upper bounds on these values in the test suite. A performance regression in the streaming path (e.g. an extra copy, a blocking sync I/O call, or a slow header parse) would go undetected. Adding a simple benchmark or a test that fires onRangeNegotiated and asserts ttfbMs is near-zero for local fake responses would catch regressions cheaply.

title: new Headers(headers) allocates a fresh Headers object on every fromByteRange call even when no extra headers are supplied
description: At line 478-479, `const mergedHeaders = new Headers(headers); mergedHeaders.set('Range', ...)` unconditionally constructs a new Headers object per request. When `headers` is undefined (the common case for unauthenticated pyramid tile fetches), this is an allocation and property-set on an empty object just to add one key. The fix is to special-case the no-headers path: `const mergedHeaders = headers ? new Headers(headers) : new Headers(); mergedHeaders.set(...)` — no semantic change, but eliminates the unnecessary spread of an undefined init dict through the Headers constructor. This is called once per tile/level fetch, so with 50+ tiles per viewport it is dozens of unnecessary allocations per frame render.
